content:
First, note that if \(\deg f=d\), then the left‐hand side
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
\]
has degree \(d\), whereas the right‐hand side
\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013x}
\]
has degree \(2d-1\).  Equating degrees forces
\[
d=2d-1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
d=1.
\]
So \(f(x)\) is linear: write
\[
f(x)=Ax+B,\quad A\neq0.
\]
Then
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
= A(x-1)+B \;+\; (Ax+B)\;+\;A(x+1)+B
=3Ax+3B,
\]
while
\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{(Ax+B)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{A^2x^2+2ABx+B^2}{2013x}
=\frac{A^2}{2013}\,x+\frac{2AB}{2013}+\frac{B^2}{2013x}.
\]
The identity
\[
3Ax+3B
=\frac{A^2}{2013}\,x+\frac{2AB}{2013}+\frac{B^2}{2013x}
\]
must hold for all \(x\neq0\).  Clearing denominators by multiplying by \(2013x\) gives
\[
6039A\,x^2+6039B\,x
\;=\;A^2x^2+2AB\,x+B^2,
\]
so the coefficients of \(x^2,x^1,x^0\) must each vanish:
\[
\begin{cases}
6039A - A^2 = 0,\\
6039B - 2AB = 0,\\
-B^2 = 0.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(A\neq0\), the first equation gives \(A=6039\).  Then the third gives \(B=0\), which also satisfies the second.  Thus
\[
f(x)=6039x,
\]
and in particular
\[
f(1)=6039.
\]
There is no other nonconstant solution, so the sum of all possible values of \(f(1)\) is

\[
\boxed{6039}.
\]
